content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 8 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 205–206 | **Source Images:** DEU_008_page_001.jpg, DEU_008_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 149 | **Images:** DEU_008_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Every commandment that I command you today you shall be careful to do, that you may live and multiply, and go in and possess the land that the LORD swore to give to your fathers.

**2** And you shall remember the whole way that the LORD your God has led you these forty years in the wilderness, that he might humble you, testing you to know what was in your heart, whether you would keep his commandments or not.

**3** And he humbled you and let you hunger and fed you with the manna, which you did not know, nor did your fathers know, that he might make you know that man does not live by bread alone, but man lives by every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD.

**4** Your clothing did not wear out on you and your foot did not swell these forty years.

**5** Know then in your heart that as a man disciplines his son, the LORD your God disciplines you.

**6** So you shall keep the commandments of the LORD your God by walking in his ways and by fearing him.

**7** For the LORD your God is bringing you into a good land, a land of brooks of water, of fountains and springs, flowing out in the valleys and the hills,

**8** a land of wheat and barley, of vines and fig trees and pomegranates, a land of olive oil and honey,

**9** a land in which you will eat bread without scarcity, in which you will lack nothing, a land whose stones are iron, and out of whose hills you can dig copper.

**10** And you shall eat and be full, and you shall bless the LORD your God for the good land he has given you.

**11** Take care lest you forget the LORD your God by not keeping his commandments and his rules and his statutes, which I command you today,

**12** lest, when you have eaten and are full and have built good houses and live in them,

**13** and when your herds and flocks multiply, and your silver and gold is multiplied, and all that you have is multiplied,

**14** then your heart be lifted up, and you forget the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery,

**15** who led you through the great and terrifying wilderness, with its fiery serpents and scorpions and thirsty ground where there was no water, who brought you water out of the flinty rock,

**16** who fed you in the wilderness with manna that your fathers did not know, that he might humble you and test you, to do you good in the end.

**17** Beware lest you say in your heart, "My power and the might of my hand have gotten me this wealth."

**18** You shall remember the LORD your God, for it is he who gives you power to get wealth, that he may confirm his covenant that he swore to your fathers, as it is this day.

**19** And if you forget the LORD your God and go after other gods and serve them and bow down to them, I solemnly warn you today that you shall surely perish.

**20** Like the nations that the LORD makes to perish before you, so shall you perish, because you would not obey the voice of the LORD your God.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Deuteronomy 8**

**"Man does not live by bread alone" (v. 3):** The Hebrew *ki lo' al-halechem levaddo yichyeh ha'adam ki al-kol-motsa pi YHWH yichyeh ha'adam* is translated with full formal equivalence. The phrase became central in the NT citation (Matthew 4:4; Luke 4:4). The LXX Vaticanus reads *ouk ep' artō monō zēsetai ho anthrōpos, all' epi panti rhēmati ekporeuomenō dia stomatos theou* (οὐκ ἐπ' ἄρτῳ μόνῳ ζήσεται ὁ ἄνθρωπος, ἀλλ' ἐπὶ παντὶ ῥήματι ἐκπορευομένῳ διὰ στόματος θεοῦ), adding "through" (*dia*) before "mouth of God," which the Matthean citation follows. The Hebrew simply has "from the mouth of the LORD," with no preposition before *pi*.

**Manna (v. 3, 16):** *man* — preserved as "manna," the traditional transliteration. The LXX has *to manna* (τὸ μαννα). Both texts note it was unknown to the fathers, emphasizing its novelty as a divine provision.

**"Humble" / 'anah (vv. 2, 3, 16):** The Pi'el of *'anah* (עָנָה) denotes to afflict, humble, or lower. The wilderness experience is repeatedly framed as divine pedagogy — humbling precedes testing (*nasah*). LXX uses *etapeinoō* (ἐταπείνωσέ), "to humble/lower," which is a faithful equivalent.

**Discipline (v. 5):** *yissar* — the parent-child discipline metaphor is central to Deuteronomy's theology of covenant. LXX has *paideusai* (παιδεῦσαι), which covers both education and discipline, a somewhat broader range than the Hebrew.

**"Flinty rock" (v. 15):** *tsur hachalamish* — literally "rock of flint." LXX has *petra akrotomou* (πέτρα ἀκρότομος), "a steep-cut/sheer rock," reflecting a different nuance — the inaccessibility of the rock rather than its mineral composition.

**Fiery serpents (v. 15):** *nachash saraph* — literally "burning/fiery serpent." The same term used in Numbers 21:6. LXX has *ophis daknōn* (ὄφιν δάκνοντα), "biting serpent," dropping the fiery quality. The Vaticanus reading here follows the LXX tradition of emphasizing the serpent's bite rather than its appearance.

**"Confirm his covenant" (v. 18):** *leha'amid et-berito* — literally "to cause his covenant to stand." "Confirm" is slightly interpretive but captures the legal force. LXX has *stēsai tēn diathēkēn autou* (στῆσαι τὴν διαθήκην), identical meaning.

**Structure:** Chapter 8 is a unified theological meditation on the meaning of the wilderness sojourn: humbling, testing, provision, and warning against pride in the land of plenty. The symmetry of humbling (vv. 2–3) and the warning against forgetting (vv. 11–18) frames the description of the good land (vv. 7–10).

— LOGOS
